title: Phulka Roti
description: Thin whole-wheat flatbreads puffed directly on an open flame, so each one balloons into a soft, smoky round. A staple at every North Indian table, phulkas take only minutes once the dough is rested.

steps.text: Combine the atta and salt in a wide bowl. Add lukewarm water a little at a time, mixing with your fingers, until a soft but non-sticky dough forms. Knead firmly for 5 minutes until the surface is smooth.
Cover the dough with a damp cloth and rest for at least 20 minutes. This relaxes the gluten so the phulkas roll thin without springing back.
Divide the rested dough into 12 equal balls. Dust the work surface lightly with flour and roll each ball into a round about 15 cm wide, keeping the thickness even so it puffs uniformly.
Heat a tawa over medium-high flame until a drop of water sizzles off instantly. Place a rolled roti on the tawa and cook for about 45 seconds until small bubbles appear on top.

Flip and cook the second side for 30 seconds until light brown specks form. Using tongs, lift the roti directly onto the open gas flame. It will puff into a ball within seconds — turn once so both sides char lightly. Remove immediately.
Stack the phulkas in a cloth-lined container. Brush lightly with ghee or butter if desired and serve at once with dal, sabzi or any gravy.

substitutions.substitute: 50% atta + 50% maida for softer rotis
substitutions.notes: pure maida phulkas won't puff as well

substitutions.substitute: cooking oil or butter brushed on top

culturalNote: The phulka is the everyday bread of North India, eaten at virtually every home lunch and dinner. Puffing it on an open flame is a skill passed from parent to child.
regionalVariation: In Punjab it is called phulka or chapati and is thicker; in UP and Delhi it is rolled paper-thin and puffed directly on the flame for a lighter result.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Aloo Paratha
description: Whole-wheat flatbreads stuffed with a spiced mashed-potato filling and cooked on a tawa with butter or ghee until crisp and golden. The definitive Punjabi breakfast, best paired with a dollop of fresh curd and tangy pickle.

steps.text: Mix atta with a pinch of salt in a bowl. Add water gradually and knead into a soft, smooth dough. Cover with a damp cloth and rest for 20 minutes.

In a bowl combine the mashed potatoes, green chilli, ginger, coriander leaves, cumin, amchur, red chilli powder, garam masala and salt. Mix well. The filling should hold its shape when pinched — if it is wet, the paratha will tear.

Divide the dough into 8 equal portions. Roll one portion into a small circle about 10 cm across. Place 2 tablespoons of filling in the centre and gather the edges upward, pinching tightly to seal. Flatten the stuffed ball gently.
Dust the sealed ball and roll it carefully into a round about 18 cm wide. Work gently and evenly so the filling stays inside. If a small tear appears, press the dough together and continue rolling.

Heat a tawa on medium-high. Place the paratha on the dry tawa and cook for 90 seconds until the underside looks dry with small brown spots. Flip, drizzle half a teaspoon of ghee around the edges, and cook another 60 seconds.

Flip once more, add another small drizzle of ghee, and press gently with a spatula to encourage even browning. The paratha is done when both sides show deep golden patches and the edges are slightly crisp. Serve immediately with curd and pickle.

substitutions.substitute: a squeeze of lemon juice mixed into the filling
butter or neutral oil
black pepper for less heat

culturalNote: Aloo paratha is the heart of the Punjabi breakfast table, typically served with a generous pat of white butter, thick curd and a spoonful of mango pickle — a combination that has fed generations of North Indian households.
regionalVariation: In Amritsar the parathas are stuffed more generously and cooked in a pool of white butter; in Delhi street stalls they are made smaller and crispier and served with a spiced potato gravy alongside.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Gobi Paratha
description: Whole-wheat flatbreads packed with a lightly spiced grated-cauliflower filling, crisped on the tawa with ghee. The key is squeezing the grated cauliflower completely dry so the filling does not make the dough soggy.

steps.text: Knead atta with salt and water into a soft, smooth dough. Cover and rest 20 minutes.

Grate the cauliflower on the fine side of a box grater. Add 0.5 tsp salt, toss, and leave for 5 minutes. Then squeeze out as much moisture as possible using your hands or a clean cloth. This step is essential — wet filling tears the paratha.
Combine the squeezed cauliflower with green chilli, ginger, coriander leaves, cumin seeds, red chilli powder, amchur and salt. Taste and adjust — the filling should be flavourful on its own.

Divide the dough into 8 balls. Roll one into a 10 cm disc, place 2 tbsp of filling in the centre, and pinch the edges shut. Flatten and roll gently to an 18 cm round, keeping the filling even throughout.

Cook on a medium-hot tawa for about 90 seconds on the first side until dry specks appear. Flip, add a drizzle of ghee, and cook another minute. Flip once more, add more ghee if needed, and press with a spatula until both sides are golden and crisp.
Serve at once with curd, butter and your favourite pickle. Gobi parathas soften quickly, so bring them straight from the tawa to the table.

substitutions.substitute: finely grated radish (mooli), squeezed dry
a squeeze of fresh lemon into the filling
butter or oil

culturalNote: Gobi paratha is a winter staple in Punjab and Haryana, made when fresh cauliflower is plentiful and cheap. Many families have a specific grating style — some prefer coarser shreds for texture.
regionalVariation: In Haryana homes the filling is often seasoned minimally — just ajwain, salt and green chilli — to let the cauliflower flavour shine. Punjabi versions add garam masala and fresh ginger for a bolder taste.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Paneer Paratha
description: Whole-wheat flatbreads filled with crumbled fresh paneer seasoned with herbs and spices, then cooked in ghee until the outside is golden and faintly crisp while the filling stays creamy. A hearty and protein-rich meal in every bite.

steps.text: Make the dough: combine atta and salt, add lukewarm water gradually, and knead into a soft smooth dough. Cover and rest for 20 minutes.
Combine crumbled paneer, green chilli, ginger, coriander leaves, cumin seeds, red chilli powder, garam masala, amchur and salt in a bowl. Mix gently — do not over-work or the paneer becomes pasty.

Divide dough into 8 balls. Roll one into a 10 cm circle. Place 2 heaped tablespoons of paneer filling in the centre, gather the dough edges up and pinch shut. Press the stuffed ball flat and roll carefully to an 18 cm round.
Place the paratha on a medium-hot tawa and cook for 90 seconds on the first side. Flip, drizzle half a teaspoon of ghee around the edges, and cook another 60 seconds.
Flip again, add a little more ghee and press lightly with a spatula. Cook until both sides show golden patches and the paratha feels slightly firm at the edges — about 2 more minutes.
Serve hot straight from the tawa with curd, mint chutney or butter. These parathas hold their heat well in a foil-lined container for the lunchbox.

substitutions.substitute: crumbled firm tofu
a squeeze of lemon juice
butter or oil

culturalNote: Paneer paratha is a festive breakfast treat across North India, often made on weekends or for special guests. Fresh homemade paneer gives the best creamy texture.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Lachha Paratha
description: A multi-layered unleavened flatbread made by rolling, folding and re-rolling the dough so it separates into delicate, flaky layers when cooked. Unlike the stuffed parathas, the layering here is all about technique — ghee between the folds creates a beautiful spiral cross-section.

ingredients.name: oil or ghee, for the dough
ghee or oil, for layering and cooking

steps.text: Mix atta, salt and 1 tbsp ghee. Add water gradually and knead into a soft, pliable dough. Cover and rest for 20 minutes — the resting is important for rolling thin without tearing.
Divide the dough into 8 balls. Roll one ball into a thin, large circle about 20 cm wide. Spread a thin, even layer of softened ghee or oil all over the surface.
Starting from one edge, make accordion pleats along the entire circle — fold about 2 cm, then fold back 2 cm, and so on, until you have a long pleated strip. The pleating creates the layers.

Coil the pleated strip tightly into a round swirl from one end to the other, tucking the end underneath. Gently flatten the coil with your palm, then roll it out carefully to a 16 cm round — do not press too hard or you will press out the layers.
Heat a tawa over medium flame. Place the paratha and cook for about 90 seconds until the underside shows light golden specks. Flip, brush ghee over the cooked side, and cook another 90 seconds.

Flip one more time, brush the second side with ghee, and cook until both sides are evenly golden with crisp edges, about 1 more minute. While still hot, use your palms to crush the paratha lightly — this separates the layers dramatically. Serve with dal makhani or paneer gravy.

substitutions.substitute: neutral oil for a dairy-free version
half atta, half maida for a more restaurant-style, flakier paratha

culturalNote: Lachha paratha is a restaurant favourite across North India, served alongside rich gravies at dhabas and wedding feasts. The layered spiral is as pleasing to look at as it is to eat.
regionalVariation: Mughlai versions are often made with maida and cooked in a tandoor, emerging with a crisp, blistered exterior. Home versions on the tawa with atta are lighter and more digestible.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Poori with Aloo Bhaji
description: Deep-fried whole-wheat pooris that puff up like balloons, served with a lightly spiced potato curry fragrant with ginger and green chilli. This classic pairing is the go-to festive breakfast and train-journey food across North India.

ingredients.name: water, just enough to form a stiff dough
potatoes, boiled, peeled and roughly mashed

steps.text: Mix atta and salt. Add just enough water to form a firm, stiff dough — stiffer than roti dough. Knead for 5 minutes, cover and rest for 15 minutes. A stiff dough makes pooris that puff up properly.

Make the aloo bhaji: heat 2 tbsp oil in a kadai over medium flame. Add cumin seeds and let them splutter. Add sliced onion and cook until translucent, about 4 minutes. Add green chilli and ginger, stir for 1 minute.

Add turmeric and the roughly mashed potatoes. Stir well to combine. Add 100 ml water, season with salt, and cook on medium-low for 5 minutes, mashing a few more times so the bhaji is slightly chunky. Finish with coriander leaves and keep warm.
Heat oil for frying in a kadai to 180°C. Divide the poori dough into 16 small balls. Roll each into a 10 cm circle — do not make them too thin or they won't puff.

Carefully slide a poori into the hot oil. Use the back of a slotted spoon to gently press it down into the oil for 2–3 seconds — this creates steam pressure that puffs the poori. It should puff within about 20 seconds.

Flip the poori once and fry for another 30 seconds until both sides are a light golden colour. Drain on paper towels. Fry all the pooris, adjusting the heat to keep the oil at 180°C. Serve immediately alongside the warm aloo bhaji.

substitutions.substitute: half atta half maida for softer pooris
sweet potato boiled and mashed for a different twist

culturalNote: Poori-bhaji is the quintessential North Indian festive breakfast, served at religious ceremonies, weddings and train canteens alike. The puffed poori is meant to be torn open immediately and dipped into the bhaji.
regionalVariation: In Uttar Pradesh the bhaji is often made without onion for religious occasions, relying on ginger and green chilli for flavour. In Maharashtra the same combination is called poha-bhaji or poori-batata bhaji.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Jeera Rice
description: Long-grain basmati rice tempered with whole cumin seeds and a touch of ghee, resulting in individual fluffy grains scented with cumin. Simple to make, endlessly versatile, and the ideal companion to any dal or North Indian gravy.

steps.text: Wash the basmati rice three times until the water runs clear, then soak in cold water for 20 minutes. This step shortens the cooking time and helps the grains stay separate. Drain before cooking.

Heat ghee in a heavy-bottomed saucepan over medium flame. Once the ghee shimmers, add cumin seeds and the bay leaf. Let them sizzle for 30 seconds until the cumin turns a shade darker and becomes aromatic.
Add the drained rice and stir gently for 1 minute to coat every grain with ghee. This toasting step adds flavour and helps the cooked grains separate.
Pour in 2.5 cups of water and add salt. Bring to a full boil over high heat, then immediately reduce to the lowest flame, cover tightly and cook for 12 minutes. Do not lift the lid during this time.
Turn off the heat and let the rice steam undisturbed with the lid on for 5 minutes. This carryover steaming finishes the cooking gently and ensures fluffy, separate grains.
Fluff gently with a fork, discarding the bay leaf. Garnish with fresh coriander leaves and serve with any dal, rajma, or paneer gravy.

substitutions.substitute: butter or neutral oil
any long-grain white rice, adjust water by 2 tbsp

culturalNote: Jeera rice is the workhorse of North Indian home cooking — quick enough for a weeknight but refined enough for guests. Restaurant versions often add a small amount of lemon juice to brighten the flavour.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Vegetable Pulao
description: Fragrant basmati rice cooked with whole spices and a colourful mix of seasonal vegetables, each grain separate and lightly coated in the spiced cooking liquid. A one-pot meal that comes together on a busy weeknight without fuss.

ingredients.name: basmati rice, soaked 20 minutes and drained
coriander and mint leaves, for garnish

steps.text: Heat oil or ghee in a heavy-bottomed pot over medium flame. Add cumin seeds, bay leaves, cinnamon, cardamom and cloves. Fry for 30 seconds until fragrant.
Add sliced onion and cook until golden brown at the edges, about 6 minutes. Stir in the ginger-garlic paste and fry for 1 minute until the raw smell disappears.
Add the carrots and beans, stir to coat in the masala, and cook for 2 minutes. Add peas and garam masala, season with salt, and mix well.
Add the soaked, drained rice and gently stir to combine with the vegetables without breaking the grains.
Pour in 2.5 cups of water, taste the water for salt, and bring to a boil. Reduce to the lowest flame, cover tightly and cook for 15 minutes. Do not uncover during cooking.
Turn off the heat and rest covered for 5 more minutes. Fluff gently with a fork, discarding the whole spices. Garnish with fresh coriander and mint, and serve with raita and pickle.

substitutions.substitute: oil for a dairy-free version
broccoli florets or cauliflower
long-grain white rice, add 2 tbsp extra water

culturalNote: Vegetable pulao is the most-cooked rice dish in North Indian homes after plain boiled rice — quick, flavourful and filling on its own or as a partner to dal. It is also a popular tiffin dish for school children.
regionalVariation: Awadhi pulao uses a touch of saffron and rose water for a more fragrant, subtle version. Kashmiri pulao includes raisins and nuts and is served at weddings.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Matar Pulao (Peas Pulao)
description: Basmati rice gently cooked with sweet green peas and whole warm spices, producing a quick, fragrant one-pot dish that pairs beautifully with any dal or raita. Minimum ingredients, maximum comfort.

ingredients.name: basmati rice, soaked 20 minutes and drained

steps.text: Heat oil in a saucepan or small pot over medium flame. Add cumin seeds, bay leaf, cinnamon and cardamom. Fry for 30 seconds until the spices are fragrant.
Add sliced onion and cook, stirring often, until pale golden — about 5 minutes. Stir in the ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute until the raw smell is gone.
Add the green peas and stir for 30 seconds to coat them in the spiced oil. If using frozen peas there is no need to thaw first.
Add the soaked, drained basmati rice and gently stir for 1 minute to toast the grains. Season with salt and pour in the water. Bring to a boil.
Once boiling, reduce to the lowest possible flame, cover tightly and cook for 12 minutes without lifting the lid.
Remove from heat and rest covered for 5 minutes. Fluff with a fork, picking out the whole spices. Garnish with coriander and serve immediately with dal tadka, curd or raita.

substitutions.substitute: corn kernels or diced carrots
regular long-grain rice, add 2 tbsp extra water

culturalNote: Matar pulao is a Monday-to-Friday dinner staple across North India — light enough not to feel heavy, flavourful enough to need nothing more than a pickle alongside. Frozen peas make it a year-round recipe.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Onion Kulcha (Tawa)
description: Soft, slightly chewy leavened flatbreads stuffed with a tangy onion and coriander filling, cooked on a tawa without a tandoor. Baking soda and curd leaven the maida dough overnight, giving kulchas their characteristic mild sourness and chew.

steps.text: Combine maida, baking soda, baking powder, sugar, salt and oil in a bowl. Add curd and mix. Add lukewarm water a little at a time to form a soft, slightly sticky dough. Knead for 5 minutes until smooth. Cover with a damp cloth and rest for at least 1 hour (or overnight in the fridge for the best flavour).

Make the filling: combine chopped onion, green chilli, coriander leaves, amchur and cumin seeds in a bowl. Add a pinch of salt and mix. Squeeze the mixture lightly so the onion releases some moisture, then drain off that moisture so the filling is not watery.

Divide the rested dough into 8 portions. Roll one into a 10 cm disc, place 2 tbsp of onion filling in the centre, gather the edges and pinch shut. Flatten gently and roll out to an oval or round about 16 cm wide — kulchas are typically oval.
Heat a heavy tawa over high flame until very hot. Wet one side of the kulcha with a little water and place the wet side down on the tawa. Press gently with the back of a spoon.

Cook for about 90 seconds until the surface looks dry and bubbles start to form. Flip and cook the second side for 60 seconds until golden brown patches appear. If your tawa allows it, invert the tawa over an open flame to char the top side lightly — this mimics the tandoor.
Brush immediately with butter and serve hot with chole, dal makhani or a simple curd. The butter is not optional — it is what makes a kulcha a kulcha.

substitutions.substitute: half atta half maida for a wholegrain version
plant-based yogurt for dairy-free
margarine or oil

culturalNote: Amritsari kulcha stuffed with onion or potato and served with white chole is one of the most iconic street foods of Punjab. The tawa version brings this restaurant classic to the home kitchen without needing a tandoor.
regionalVariation: Amritsari kulchas are cooked on the inner wall of a tandoor using a damp cloth, giving them a signature blistered, charred base. The tawa method is a practical home adaptation.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Quick Pressure-Cooker Vegetable Biryani
description: A weeknight-friendly biryani made by cooking spiced vegetables and soaked basmati rice together in a pressure cooker for a single whistle, then resting off the heat so the grains steam to perfection. Full biryani flavour in under 35 minutes.

ingredients.name: basmati rice, soaked 20 minutes and drained
whole garam masala (bay leaf, cinnamon, cardamom, cloves)

steps.text: Heat ghee in the pressure cooker base over medium-high flame. Add the whole garam masala ingredients (cinnamon stick 1 inch, 3 cardamom pods, 3 cloves, 1 bay leaf) and fry for 30 seconds until fragrant.
Add sliced onion and fry until deep golden brown, about 7 minutes, stirring frequently. A well-browned onion is the base of all biryani flavour. Reserve half the fried onion to sprinkle on top later.

Add ginger-garlic paste to the remaining onion and fry for 1 minute. Add chopped tomato, red chilli powder, turmeric and garam masala. Stir and cook until the tomato is soft and the oil begins to separate, about 3 minutes.
Beat the curd with a pinch of salt and add to the pot. Stir over medium heat for 1 minute until the curd is absorbed. Add the diced potato and carrot, peas, mint and coriander and mix.

Add the soaked, drained rice and 2 cups of water (the water should be well-salted). Stir gently once. Secure the pressure cooker lid and cook on high until you get exactly 1 whistle, then immediately reduce to the lowest flame and cook for 5 more minutes.

Turn off the heat and let the pressure release naturally for 10 minutes — do not force-release or the rice will clump. Open the lid, fluff gently, scatter the reserved fried onion on top and serve with raita and pickle.

substitutions.substitute: neutral oil
coconut milk (3 tbsp) for a different flavour profile
cauliflower florets or corn

culturalNote: The pressure cooker biryani is a celebrated shortcut in Indian home kitchens — a way to produce rice layered with vegetable masala on a school night without the hours of dum cooking.
regionalVariation: Traditional dum biryani uses separate layers of rice and masala cooked slowly over a sealed pot; this one-pot pressure version is more common in everyday North Indian homes.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Dal Khichdi
description: A comforting one-pot dish of rice and moong dal cooked together until soft and porridge-like, finished with a crackling ghee tadka. Khichdi is what every North Indian home cooks when someone is under the weather or the fridge is nearly empty.

steps.text: Rinse the rice and moong dal together two or three times until the water runs mostly clear. Place both in a pressure cooker with 4 cups of water, turmeric and salt.
Close the lid and cook on high heat. After the first whistle, reduce to medium and cook for 2 more whistles — 3 whistles total. Turn off the heat and let the pressure release naturally.

Open the cooker and stir the khichdi — it should be soft and cohesive, not dry. If it looks too thick, add a splash of hot water and stir over low heat. The consistency should be like a thick risotto.

Prepare the tadka: heat ghee in a small pan over medium-high flame until shimmering. Add cumin seeds, dried red chilli and asafoetida. Fry for 30 seconds until the cumin crackles and the chilli darkens.
Add grated ginger if using and fry for 10 seconds. Pour the entire sizzling tadka over the khichdi immediately. The sound of sizzling fat hitting the warm dal is deeply satisfying.
Stir the tadka into the khichdi, top with coriander leaves, and serve with a drizzle of extra ghee, a wedge of lemon and papad alongside. Plain curd or pickle completes the meal.

substitutions.substitute: masoor dal (red lentil) or toor dal
oil, but ghee tadka is what gives khichdi its signature taste

culturalNote: Khichdi is revered in Indian culture as the original comfort food — it is the first solid food given to babies, the prescription when one is ill, and the celebratory dish of Makar Sankranti in some states.
regionalVariation: Gujarat adds peanuts and sesame to the tadka; UP cooks it drier and serves it with ghee poured on top at the table. Biharis make a coarser, more textured version with whole green moong.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Palak Khichdi
description: A nutritious green twist on the classic dal khichdi, with fresh spinach puree stirred into the cooked rice-dal base for a vivid emerald colour and a mildly earthy flavour. A complete, iron-rich meal the whole family will enjoy.

steps.text: Briefly blanch the spinach in boiling salted water for 1 minute, then drain and transfer to a blender with 2 tbsp water. Blend to a smooth puree and set aside. Blanching locks in the green colour.

Rinse rice and moong dal together until the water runs clear. Heat ghee in a pressure cooker over medium flame. Add cumin seeds and let them splutter, then add onion and cook until translucent, about 3 minutes.
Add ginger-garlic paste and green chilli and cook for 1 minute until the raw smell disappears. Add turmeric, mix, then add the rinsed rice and dal. Stir for 1 minute to coat with the ghee.
Add 3.5 cups of water and salt. Close the lid and cook on high until you get 2 whistles, then lower the heat and cook for 1 more whistle. Turn off and let the pressure drop naturally.

Open the cooker, stir gently. The khichdi should be soft and porridge-like. If too thick, add a splash of hot water. Stir in the spinach puree and garam masala over low heat and cook for 2 minutes until heated through.
Adjust salt, spoon into bowls and finish with a drizzle of ghee. Serve with curd, papad and a wedge of lemon. This is a wholesome, balanced meal as it is.

substitutions.substitute: fenugreek leaves (methi) for a slightly bitter twist
masoor dal (red lentil)
oil

culturalNote: Palak khichdi is a popular lunchtime dish for children across North India — the spinach hides behind the mild, comforting base so even picky eaters enjoy it. Many mothers make it as a weekly fixture.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Besan Chilla
description: Savoury gram-flour pancakes spiced with cumin, ajwain and green chilli, cooked thin on a hot tawa until golden at the edges. High in protein, gluten-free and ready in minutes, besan chilla is a beloved quick breakfast across North India.

ingredients.name: water, to make a thin batter

steps.text: Sift besan into a bowl. Add cumin seeds, ajwain, turmeric, red chilli powder and salt. Whisk in water gradually, adding a little at a time, until you get a smooth batter the consistency of thin cream — no lumps. The batter should pour easily from a spoon.
Stir in the chopped onion, tomato, green chilli and coriander leaves. Let the batter rest for 5 minutes while you heat the tawa.

Heat a non-stick tawa or flat pan over medium-high flame. Lightly oil the surface. Pour a ladleful (about 4 tbsp) of batter into the centre and quickly spread it in a circular motion using the back of the ladle to a 15 cm round.

Drizzle a few drops of oil around the edges. Cook for 2 minutes until the edges look set and the surface on top appears dry. Flip carefully using a flat spatula — the chilla should release cleanly from the pan.
Cook the second side for 1 minute until it shows golden patches. The chilla is done when both sides are evenly golden and it has a slight crisp at the edges while staying soft in the centre.
Serve immediately with green chutney, mint curd or tomato ketchup. Leftover batter keeps in the fridge for up to two days — just re-stir before using.

substitutions.substitute: moong dal batter (soaked and ground moong dal) for a lighter texture
grated paneer stirred into the batter for a protein-rich version

culturalNote: Besan chilla is a staple quick breakfast in Rajasthan, Delhi and UP, popular because besan (gram flour) is cheap, protein-rich and always in the pantry. It predates the modern concept of high-protein breakfasts by centuries.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Stuffed Capsicum (Bharwa Shimla Mirch)
description: Hollowed-out green and red capsicums filled with a spiced potato-pea mixture, then shallow-fried on the tawa until the skin blisters and the filling turns golden. A show-stopping yet simple side dish that elevates any roti or rice meal.

ingredients.name: medium capsicum (bell peppers), tops cut and deseeded

steps.text: Heat 1 tbsp oil in a small pan. Add cumin seeds, then the chopped onion and green chilli. Cook until the onion is soft, about 3 minutes. Add ginger and fry for 30 seconds more.

Add the mashed potato, boiled peas, red chilli powder, amchur and garam masala. Mix well and cook for 2 minutes. Season with salt. Stir in coriander leaves. The filling should hold its shape when pressed. Set aside to cool slightly.
Rub the inside of each hollowed capsicum with a pinch of salt. Fill each one firmly with the potato-pea mixture, packing it to the top. Replace the cut tops as lids if desired.

Heat 2 tbsp oil in a wide, heavy kadai or non-stick pan over medium flame. Place the stuffed capsicums upright in the pan. Cover and cook for 8 minutes, turning them every 2 minutes so all sides get colour.
Remove the lid, increase the heat slightly and cook for 3 more minutes until the skin blisters and darkens in patches. The capsicum should be soft but still hold its shape.

Arrange on a plate, garnish with a few coriander leaves, and serve alongside phulka or plain rice and dal. The sweet-spicy contrast of the pepper skin and savoury potato filling is the appeal of this dish.

substitutions.substitute: paneer crumbled or a potato-paneer mix for a richer filling
corn kernels
a squeeze of lemon juice into the filling

culturalNote: Bharwa shimla mirch is a popular side dish at North Indian weddings and festive meals. The trick is choosing capsicums that can stand upright on their own so the filling does not fall out during cooking.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Seviyan Kheer
description: Thin wheat vermicelli slow-simmered in full-fat milk with sugar, cardamom and rose water, finished with golden raisins and slivered almonds. This fragrant, milky pudding is a classic Eid dessert as well as an everyday sweet treat across North India.

steps.text: Heat ghee in a heavy-bottomed pot or saucepan over medium flame. Add the raisins and almonds (and cashews if using) and fry for 1 minute until the raisins puff and the nuts turn a light golden colour. Remove with a slotted spoon and set aside.
In the same pot, add the vermicelli and roast over medium heat, stirring constantly, for 2–3 minutes until it turns a light golden colour and smells nutty. Do not let it darken too much.
Pour in the milk and raise the heat to bring it to a boil. Stir frequently to prevent the milk sticking to the bottom of the pot.

Once the milk boils, reduce to medium-low and simmer uncovered for 15–20 minutes, stirring every 2 minutes, until the vermicelli is completely soft and the milk has thickened noticeably. The kheer should coat the back of a spoon.
Add sugar and stir until it dissolves. Simmer for 2 more minutes. Turn off the heat and stir in the cardamom powder and rose water.
The kheer will thicken more as it cools. Serve warm, at room temperature, or chilled — all versions are delicious. Top each portion with the fried nuts and raisins just before serving.

substitutions.substitute: rice vermicelli for a gluten-free version
coconut milk for a dairy-free version, use 700 ml with 300 ml water
condensed milk (reduce quantity of sugar and milk)

culturalNote: Seviyan kheer is prepared across Muslim households throughout India on Eid ul-Fitr morning, making it one of the most emotionally resonant dishes in the North Indian sweet repertoire. In many homes it is the first thing eaten after the morning prayers.
regionalVariation: In Lucknow (Awadh), the kheer is cooked with saffron strands and extra rose water for a heady floral aroma. In Hyderabad a slightly different version called sheer khurma includes dates and is thicker.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen
